coredata.dc:title: Template-assisted Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages as biomimetic peroxidase: A multi-colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence separated-type immunosensor for the detection of ochratoxin A
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Hongshuai
Cai, Yue
Qileng, Aori
Quan, Zhu
Zeng, Wei
He, Kaiyu
Liu, Yingju
coredata.dc:description: The convenient and effective detection of toxins is urgently demanded for food security and human health. Herein, based on the catalytic activity of mimetic peroxidase from the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages, a dual-modal multi-colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor for the sensitive detection of ochratoxin A (OTA) was successfully developed. For the multi-colorimetric detection, H2O2 can be effectively decomposed by Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 to form ·OH groups, thus Au nanorods (Au NRs) can be etched to exhibit vivid color variations and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) shifts. For the ratiometric fluorescence detection, o-phenylenediamine was oxidized by Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) in the presence of H2O2. Interestingly, the exogenous fluorescence signal source of carbon dots can be quenched by DAP via inner filter effect, while a new emission peak at 563 nm can be discovered, forming a ratiometric fluorescence signal. Due to the independent signals and mutual confirmation, the performance of the dual-modal immunosensor for the detection of OTA was significantly improved, where a broad linear range from 1 ng/L to 10 μg/L with a detection limit of 0.56 ng/L (S/N = 3) was achieved. The sensing strategy was also used to monitor OTA in millet and lake water samples with a satisfied performance.

head.ce:title: Template-assisted Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages as biomimetic peroxidase: A multi-colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence separated-type immunosensor for the detection of ochratoxin A

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The convenient and effective detection of toxins is urgently demanded for food security and human health. Herein, based on the catalytic activity of mimetic peroxidase from the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages, a dual-modal multi-colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor for the sensitive detection of ochratoxin A (OTA) was successfully developed. For the multi-colorimetric detection, H2O2 can be effectively decomposed by Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 to form ·OH groups, thus Au nanorods (Au NRs) can be etched to exhibit vivid color variations and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) shifts. For the ratiometric fluorescence detection, o-phenylenediamine was oxidized by Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) in the presence of H2O2. Interestingly, the exogenous fluorescence signal source of carbon dots can be quenched by DAP via inner filter effect, while a new emission peak at 563 nm can be discovered, forming a ratiometric fluorescence signal. Due to the independent signals and mutual confirmation, the performance of the dual-modal immunosensor for the detection of OTA was significantly improved, where a broad linear range from 1 ng/L to 10 μg/L with a detection limit of 0.56 ng/L (S/N = 3) was achieved. The sensing strategy was also used to monitor OTA in millet and lake water samples with a satisfied performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins, secondary metabolites product produced by filamentous fungi, can remain in foodstuffs for their thermal chemical stability and hence bring enormous health risks to animals and humans (Lin et al., 2016, 2017). Up to present, according to the carcinogenic risk of the mycotoxins contamination, several mycotoxins have been reported and classified into various categories (Zhou and Tang, 2020). Among them, ochratoxins constitute a kind of mycotoxins with similar structures produced mainly by Penicillium and Aspergillus species, including ochratoxin A (OTA), ochratoxin B (OTB), and ochratoxin C (OTC), respectively (Wei et al., 2019). OTA is one of the most widely distributed and most harmful to human health (Liu et al., 2020). Therefore, the World Health Organization (WHO) explicitly defines that the human body’s tolerable weekly intake is 100 ng/kg (Rivas et al., 2015). Especially, OTA is regarded as a potential carcinogen (Group 2B) for human by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) (Jiang et al., 2018). Consequently, the development of selective, sensitive and rapid detection of OTA is of great importance for human health and food security.

Until now, various traditional techniques for OTA detection have been proposed, such as liquid/gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry and high performance liquid chromatography (Amelin et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2014; Irakli et al., 2017). Nevertheless, due to the expensive instruments, trained personnel, and time consuming, these methods cannot be extensively applied. Recently, colorimetric immunoassay has become a promising strategy since visual judgment can be realized by naked eyes without the need of complicated equipment (Gao et al., 2015; Ren et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019). For example, we recently fabricated a colorimetric immunosensor that utilize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as the chromogenic substrate for visual detection of OTA (Zhu et al., 2020). Lin’s group developed a liposome-based colorimetric aptasensor to achieve the sensing of OTA, which exhibited a visible color variation at different target amounts (Lin et al., 2018). In particular, gold nanorods (Au NRs)-based colorimetric immunosensors have gained extensive favor for the multicolor variations and tunable aspect ratios (Hauwiller et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020), such as Tang’ group fabricated a high-resolution multi-colorimetric sensor with Au NRs for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with bare eyes (Gao et al., 2014).

Although colorimetric sensors have some intrinsic strengths in the convenience and visual detection, the specificity and sensitivity are still critical factors restricting its application. Hence, in order to enhance the accuracy of immunosensors, the dual-readout immunoassay has been developed due to separate response signals and transmission mechanisms (Campu et al., 2018; Mei et al., 2018; Xiong et al., 2018; Miao et al., 2019). The performance of proposed immunoassay has been significantly improved due to the completely independent signal paths and mutually confirmed test results. Recently, the ratiometric fluorescence method, which can record two emission signals at one excitation wavelength, has been used in cell imaging or trace detection of metal ions and biomolecules (Zou et al., 2016; Jin et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020). The built-in self-calibration capability of ratiometric fluorescence can enhance the reliability and precision. Hu’s group (Ma et al., 2017) developed a ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy based on the exogenous fluorescence signal response of N, Cu-codoped carbon dots (N/Cu CDs), which can be effectively quenched by 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP, the oxidative product of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) from horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-induced peroxidase catalytic reaction) owing to the inner filter effect (IFE). In addition, DAP exhibits strong emission at 557 nm under the same excitation wavelength, emerging a typical ratiometric fluorescence response. Based on the same theory, Jin’s group (Jin et al., 2019) reported a ratiometric fluorescent biosensor from silicon quantum dots to detect organophosphate pesticides.

The enzyme activity is important in the construction of the dual-modal immunosensor platform. Unfortunately, owing to the strict storage environment and the costly preparation of natural protein enzymes, their application is greatly limited. To address such drawbacks, efforts have been made to develop and explore potential alternatives of enzymes (Farka et al., 2018; Miao et al., 2018). Among them, nano-enzymes have become a promising candidate due to their extraordinary merits, such as low cost, easy preparation, multiple functions, and high stability (Chen et al., 2020). Notably, hollow nanostructures are perfect mimetic enzyme for their abundant binding sites and substrate channels for catalytic reaction, mainly ascribing to the characteristic internal voids, thin shells, and great specific surface area (Qi et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018). In addition to structure control, the chemical composition of nano-enzymes also shows a striking influence on catalytic capability, since they can combine the properties of each nanocomposite and exhibit a synergistically enhanced mimic enzymatic activity. For example, Yang’s group (Yang et al., 2020) demonstrated that the existence of Cu+ in reaction system is conducive to accelerating the Fe2+/Fe3+ conversion and decomposing H2O2 to produce ·OH and ·O2 ¯. Similar phenomena were also discovered in CeO2/NT-TiO2 and Fe NPs@Co3O4 hollow nanocages (Zhao et al., 2015, 2018).

Herein, according to Pearson’s hard and soft acid-base principle, we developed a facile template-assisted strategy for fabricating uniform Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages by employing Cu2O nanocubes as the sacrificial templates. Compared with single Cu2O, the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 presented higher mimetic enzyme catalytic activity. Meanwhile, Au NRs were prepared by double surfactant assisted gold seed inducing growth strategy, while carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-step microwave hydrothermal method. In this study, we firstly fabricated a Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-induced dual-mode multi-colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor for the sensitive detection of OTA. After the specific immune response, for the colorimetric assay, the OTA concentration can be conveniently and intuitively distinguished from the color variations, since Au NRs can be etched by Cu2O@Fe(OH)3. For the ratiometric fluorescence, Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 can efficiently oxidize nonfluorescent OPD into DAP with a yellow light emission at 563 nm in the presence of H2O2 and also an effective quench of the CDs emission peak at 424 nm, forming a ratiometric fluorescence signal. Ultimately, the marriage of colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor showed excellent detection performance for OTA due to their independent response and mutual confirmation.

Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW: 40,000) and collagens polypeptide were bought from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co. Ltd, while dopamine hydrochloride and Tween were from Acros Organics, Shanghai. Ascorbic acid (AA), CuCl2·2H2O, NaOH, HCl, sodium acetate, sodium thiosulfate, sodium citrate, AgNO3, H2O2 (30%), and NaBH4 were from Aladdin biological technology Co. Ltd, while OPD was from Tianjin Damao chemical reagent factory, and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai. HAuCl4, FeCl2·4H2O, hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC) and 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTMS) was from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. The secondary goat anti-rabbit antibody (Ab2, 1 mg/mL) was from Santa Cruz. The antigen of OTA (5 mg/mL) and the antibody of OTA (Ab1, 1 mg/mL) were from College of Food Sciences, South China Agricultural University. The PBST was prepared by mixing 0.01 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) with 0.5% tween-20 and used as the washing buffer. The blocking solution was obtained by dissolving 0.1 g collagens polypeptide into 10 mL PBST (0.01 M).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Talos F200S, Thermo Fisher, America), automatic rapid specific surface area and porosity analyzer (ASAP-2460, Micromeritics, America), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos L120C, Thermo Fisher, America) were used to characterize the morphologies of nanomaterials, while Fourier Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet IS 10, Thermo Fisher, America), X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/max-IIIA, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, America) were used to characterize the structure and element analysis of nanomaterials. The UV-visible spectrophotometer (Evolution 220, Thermo Fisher, America) was used to record the absorption spectrum, while the fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) was used to record the fluorescence spectra and an iPhone 6s plus was used to take photos of the color changes in the reaction wells.

Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanostructures were prepared according to Fig. 1A. After Cu2O nanocubes were synthesized according to earlier work (Zhang et al., 2009), Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanostructures were prepared by removing the cubic Cu2O template and precipitating iron ions simultaneously. Briefly, PVP (MW: 40,000, 1.665 g) was dispersed in 50 mL of water, then cubic Cu2O templates (0.025 g) and FeCl2·4H2O (0.0105 g) were added. After the mixture was stirred for 10 min, 0.6 M Na2S2O3 (6 mL) was dropwise added at a constant rate and reacted for another 15 min. As shown in Fig. 1B, when the accumulation of Fe(OH)3 reached a certain amount, the Fe(OH)3 shell can be formed on Cu2O nanocubes. The etching time can influence the size of Cu2O nanocubes, showing the size decreased progressively with the time, while the morphology of tetragonal was maintained. Thus, a yolk-shell nanostructure was formed, while the suspension color was changed from brick red to burgundy (Fig. S1). The final product was stored in methanol (10 mL).

Before the association with Ab2, Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 was aminated by adding 7.2 mL of the above solution to ethanol (35.6 mL) and treating with APTMS (0.2 mL). The mixture was stirred at 30 °C for 1 day, centrifuged, and redispersed in Tris-HAc solution (10 mM). After ultrasonicating aminated Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 (2 mL) for 20 min, Ab2 (1 mg/mL, 4 μL) was added and stirred at 4 °C for 1 day. The product was centrifuged and redispersed in PBS (2 mL) for further use. Therefore, Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-Ab2 was obtained by the covalent binding between amino groups of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 with carboxyl groups of Ab2.

The kinetic parameters of Cu2O and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 were carried out by recording the UV-vis absorption intensity change of the mixture at 420 nm with time. In HAc-NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH = 6), a fixed amount of H2O2 was taken to investigate the kinetic parameters of OPD (0.98–9.64 mM) under the same concentration of Cu2O or Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 (180 μM). Subsequently, the concentration of OPD was unchanged while altering that of H2O2 (1.61–16.13 mM). The kinetic constant was calculated on the basis of Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot originated from Michaelis-Menten equation as below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Au NRs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The double surfactant-assisted gold seed-induced growth strategy was used to prepare Au NRs. Firstly, the gold seed solution was synthesized. HAuCl4 (5 mL, 0.5 mM) was mixed with CTAB (5 mL, 0.2 M) in a 15 mL sample bottle, followed by adding ice-cold NaBH4 (0.6 mL, 10 mM) under vigorous stirring. After the color was changed to pale brown, the solution was aged for 1.5 h without heating. Secondly, the growth solution was prepared by dissolving sodium oleate (NaOL, 0.25 g) and CTAB (1.4 g) in water (50 mL). Subsequently, AgNO3 (3 mL, 4 mM) was added and stirred for 15 min, followed by adding HAuCl4 (50 mL, 1 mM) and HCl (0.3 mL, 12 M) and stirring for 30 min. Then, ascorbic acid (0.7 mL, 79 mM) was added and stirred for 1.5 min until the solution became colorless. Ultimately, 0.12 mL of the obtained gold seed solution was added and kept stable overnight at 30 °C for Au NRs growing. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm, the precipitates were redispersed in 1 mM CTAB solution.

By microwave heating strategy, the fluorescent CDs were prepared by dissolving 1.5 g of glutathione (GSH) into 6 mL of water, and heating for 5 min using a domestic microwave oven over medium heat (400 W, 2450 MHz). The color of the solution can be altered from canary yellow to chocolate-brown, while the moisture was basically evaporated. Subsequently, the resultant products were redissolved in water. After filtration with a needle-cylinder membrane filter (0.22 µm), the transparent aqueous solution was transferred into dialysis bag (Mw = 300 Da) for 48 h to remove the overreacted residue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The assembly of the dual-modal immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, dopamine (50 μL, 1 mg/mL) was added into the wells of the microplate. After incubating at 37 °C for 0.5 h, the OTA antigen (30 μL, 10 μg/mL) was added into each well and incubated at 4 °C for 2 h, followed by adding the blocking solution (30 μL) to seal up the rest sites. Then, 30 μL of the mixture solution including different concentrations of OTA and a fixed volume of Ab1 (5 μg/mL) were injected into the wells, and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h (After each step of reaction, the microplate wells were washed with PBST and dried by N2). After that, 30 μL of the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-Ab2 solution was added and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, and the unconjugated solution was collected. After each well was further washed with 40 μL of PBST, the solution was also collected and mixed with the above unconjugated solution for the next step.

Secondly, for the multi-colorimetric detection, 30 μL of the above mixture was transferred into the microplate, then 40 μL of HCl (0.3 M), 50 μL of H2O2 (0.5 M) and 200 μL of Au NRs were sequentially injected. After reacting for 20 min at 50 °C, the color of the mixture was changed, while the color and the absorption spectra were recorded by an iPhone 6s plus and a UV-visible spectrophotometer, respectively. In addition, for the ratiometric fluorescence detection, 30 μL of the above mixture was transferred into the microplate, then 150 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2 M), 40 μL of OPD (0.6 M), 50 μL of H2O2 (0.5 M), and 150 μL of CDs were sequentially injected, and reacted for 10 min at room temperature, inducing the color change from OPD (colorless) to DAP (yellow). The ratiometric fluorescent intensity was recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer.

Fig. 2A-B showed a typical TEM images of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages consisting of a Cu2O core and a complete Fe(OH)3 shell. Compared with Cu2O cubes (Fig. S2B), the core of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 basically maintained the cubic shape of Cu2O, while the average particle size decreased from 500 nm to 320 nm. Meanwhile, the regular Fe(OH)3 shell structure was formed on the outer side of Cu2O core. The results confirmed the successful synthesis of the yolk-shell nanostructure. The morphological changes were also mapped with the color variations of the reaction solution (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 2C and D, the shell of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 consisted of ultrathin nanosheets. Unlike the smooth surface of Cu2O nanocubes (Figs. S2A and 2B), the surface of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 exhibited a scaly fold structure. As in N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. S3), compared to Cu2O nanocubes, the BET surface area of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 enormously increased from 1.78 to 131.02 m2/g, thus exposing more active binding sites. Simultaneously, the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages with an average pore width of 12.72 nm (inset of Fig. S3B). Therefore, the adhesion ability of the biomarkers and the reaction rate with the substrate were significantly enhanced. Additionally, the comparison of elemental mappings of Cu2O cubes (Fig. S2E) and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages (Fig. 2E) also revealed that the yolk-shell structure was formed. In particular, it can be clearly demonstrated from the overlapped elemental mapping that Cu element was intensively distributed in the core of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages. The element of Fe was evenly distributed around the core, and the element of O was situated at the whole nanocage. Energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) was further used to characterize the surface of Cu2O nanocubes and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages. As shown in Fig. S2D and Fig. 2F, elements including Cu and O can be found in Cu2O and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3, while Fe can be only found in Cu2O@Fe(OH)3. In Fig. 2G, the XRD pattern implied that the peaks at 73.5°, 61.3°, 42.3°, 36.4°, and 29.6° can be ascribed to the (311), (220), (200), (111), and (110) crystal planes of a cubic Cu2O (JCPDS 05–0667). No additional diffraction peaks were found in the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages, which indicated that the formed Fe(OH)3 shell cannot change the crystal form of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3.

The chemical composition and oxidation states of Cu2O nanocubes and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages were further studied by XPS. Fig. S4A and Fig. 3A showed the survey spectra of Cu2O and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3, where three distinct peaks with binding energy at 930.4, 528.5 and 282.5 belonged to Cu 2p, O 1s and C 1s, respectively. Additionally, two additional peaks about 710 eV were found in Cu2O@Fe(OH)3, which could be attributed to Fe 2p1 and Fe 2p3. To further demonstrate the oxidation state of the elements in Cu2O@Fe(OH)3, the XPS spectra of O 1s, Fe 2p, and Cu 2p of Cu2O and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 were compared in Fig. S4 and Fig. 3. The major peaks at 932.2 eV and 952.2 eV in the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 should be ascribed to Cu+, the peaks at 954.1 eV and 934.1 eV reflected the partially oxidized Cu2+, and the peaks around 942.2 and 962.2 eV were satellite peaks (Zahran et al., 2014) (Fig. 3B and Fig. S4B). According to the previous study (Biesinger et al., 2011), the major peaks at 711.1 eV and 724.6 eV in the Fe 2p of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages corresponded to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 (Fig. 3C), whereas the satellite peaks can be found at 716.2 and 731.6 eV, respectively, indicating the presence of Fe(OH)3. As for the O 1s spectrum, the peaks at 530.1 and 531.3 eV were attributed to the oxygen species of surface hydroxyl groups and lattice oxygen of CuO and Cu2O in Cu2O cubes, respectively (Yao et al., 2010) (Fig. S4C). However, the peaks of CuO and Cu2O in Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages exhibited a negative shift relative to that of Cu2O nanocubes. The changes of binding energy indicated that the charge was redistributed owing to the interaction between Fe(OH)3 shell and Cu2O core in Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages, which could dramatically enhance the catalytic activity (Huang et al., 2020). The enhanced peak at 531.7 eV demonstrated the appearance of the ‘ions OH-’, while the peak located at 532.8 eV can be assigned to the weakly adsorbed oxygen species (Nai et al., 2013) (Fig. 3D). Generally, Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages was successfully prepared.

The amination of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 was characterized by FTIR (Fig. S5). Compared with the original characteristic peak of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3, the amino-functionalized Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 showed new absorption bands at 2968 cm−1 and 1482 cm−1 from stretching and deformation vibrations of C-H, respectively. The other two characteristic bands at 1082 cm−1 and 952 cm−1 were from C-NH2 stretching vibration and the N-H deformation vibration, respectively. Afterwards, the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-Ab2 was further investigated by the UV-visible spectra. In Fig. 2H, a characteristic absorption of protein at about 280 nm can be ascribed to the peak of Ab2 (Larson et al., 2018). Although no peak can be found in the spectrum of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3, the absorption peak of protein was detected in the spectrum of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-Ab2. Therefore, it can be concluded that the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-Ab2 has been successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S6, OPD was employed as the chromogenic substrate to compare the peroxidase-like activity of Cu2O nanocubes and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages. OPD (colorless) could be catalyzed and oxidized into DAP (yellow) by Cu2O nanocubes or Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages with the aid of H2O2, demonstrating both Cu2O and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 had peroxidase mimic activities. Furthermore, after the addition of Cu2O or Cu2O@Fe(OH)3, the absorption at 420 nm changed in the OPD-H2O2 system, while the absorption intensity in the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-OPD-H2O2 system was higher than that in the Cu2O-OPD-H2O2. These results reflected that the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages had higher peroxidase catalytic activity than Cu2O cubes.

To further compare the catalytic activity, the kinetic parameters with different concentrations of OPD and H2O2 as substrates were carried out in the presence of Cu2O cubes or Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages. Fig. 4 displayed that the catalytic behaviors of Cu2O or Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 were consistent with the Michaelis-Menten model. The catalytic including Vm ax and Km were calculated from the double-reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plots. The Km value for Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 with OPD as the substrate was 0.90 mM, indicating that Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 possessed a higher affinity with OPD. Notably, the Km of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 with H2O2 as the substrate was 1.35 mM, lower than that of Cu2O (2.08 mM) and HRP (3.70 mM) (Xia et al., 2015), demonstrating that less H2O2 is needed to achieve the catalytic activity. The reasons for the high catalytic activity of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 as follows. First, the synergistic effect of Cu2O core and Fe(OH)3 shell should be considered (Yang et al., 2020). In addition, Sun et al. (Sun et al., 2019) demonstrated that Cu+ can effectively decompose H2O2 to produce ·OH through Fenton reaction. As in Scheme 1, the conversion of Cu+ /Cu2+ -based Fenton reaction and the reduction reaction of Fe3+/Fe2+ would further enhance the production of ·OH [Eq. 1, 2, 4, and 5]. The reduction of Cu2+ by H2O2 [Eq. 2, k 2 = 4.6 × 102 M−1 S−1] is faster than that of Fe3+ by H2O2 [Eq. 3, k 4 = (0.1–2) × 102 M−1 S−1] (Zhang et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2018), which loosens the limited rate step. Therefore, the yolk-shell of Cu2O and Fe(OH)3 play a coordinate role in peroxidase-like catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Au NRs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM micrographs of the Au NRs were shown in Fig. 5A, B, and C. The monodispersed Au NRs exhibited a rod-shaped structure with consistency of thickness. From Fig. 5D, it was clearly observed that more than 80% of Au NRs were mainly distributed over 65–75 nm in the length. Furthermore, the wavelength and intensity of plasmon peak exhibited negligible changes within 4 weeks storage, indicating the long-term stability of the Au NRs (Fig. 5E).

As in the TEM images in Fig. 6A and B, the monodispersed CDs showed a uniform dewdrop-like shape, while the average size ranged from 1.75 nm to 3.25 nm (The particle diameter distribution of CDs was measured and calculated from the TEM images using the Nano Measure Software, Fig. 6C). A lattice spacing of 0.236 nm was clearly noticed (Fig. 6B), which may be similar to the (100) facet of graphite (Zhao et al., 2017). Fig. 6D showed the FTIR spectrum of the CDs. A broad and strong absorption band at 3446 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H bonds, while the characteristic absorption at 2923 cm−1 and 1378 cm−1 were from the stretching vibration and deformation vibration of C–H, respectively (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2015). The peak at 1025 cm−1 was from the C–O stretching vibration, while the peak at 1633 cm−1 belonged to the C˭O vibration (Zhao et al., 2017). Furthermore, the XRD spectrum (Fig. 6E) demonstrated a broad band at 25.3°, which attributed to the (002) plane of graphite carbon nitride. As shown in Fig. 6F, the maximum emission and excitation wavelengths of CDs were 424 nm and 360 nm, respectively. From the inset in Fig. 6A, under ultraviolet light irradiation, the CDs showed a bright blue fluorescence. Therefore, CDs were successfully prepared.

Scheme 2 depicts the principle of the dual-modal multi-colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor platform for OTA detection. First, the antigen was coated in the 96-well plates by dopamine, followed by the addition of mixture containing a certain amount of Ab1 and different concentrations of OTA. Consequently, the fixed antigen and free OTA could competitively bind to Ab1. The addition of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-Ab2 can form the antigen-Ab1-Ab2 sandwiched structure. After the immune response, the multi-colorimetric detection was achieved by Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-guided etching of Au NRs. Different morphologies and sizes of Au NRs were formed, accompanied by colorful color changes and blue shifts LSPR of Au NRs. The corresponding TEM images of the etching process were further confirmed the feasibility of the principle (Fig. S7), showing the length of Au NRs was gradually shortened while the diameter remained essentially unchanged. The inset pictures showed the color transitions of the corresponding mixture. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescence detection was based on Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 with the peroxidase-like activity for the catalytic oxidation of OPD. Interestingly, DAP, the oxidation product of OPD can efficiently quench the fluorescence of CDs, while DAP exhibited an obviously fluorescence emission peak at 563 nm, thus a typical ratiometric fluorescent signal can be formed. It was noteworthy that the fluorescence quenching effect of DAP on CDs may be due to the inner filter effect (IFE), since the emission or excitation wavelength of a fluorophore can be overlapped with the absorption wavelength of other light-absorbing substances, causing the quench of the emission intensity of the fluorophore (Fig. S8) (Cai et al., 2020).

The feasibility of the designed dual-modal multi-colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence immunosensor was investigated. As for the multi-colorimetric detection, two extinction peaks of Au NRs can be found at 703 nm and 521 nm in the UV-vis spectrum (Fig. S9A), corresponding to the longitudinal plasmon band and transverse plasmon band, respectively. Subsequently, after individual introducing HCl and H2O2 to Au NRs, the longitudinal LSPR peak of Au NRs did not shift, indicating that the unsuccessful etching of Au NRs. But when HCl, H2O2, and Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 were simultaneously appended, which could induce the obvious LSPR blue-shift of Au NRs, demonstrating that the successful etching of Au NRs. For the ratiometric fluorescence detection (Fig. S9B), the emission peak at 563 nm only appeared when Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 existed in the system. In addition, the fluorescence peak emission of CDs at 424 nm decreased significantly, implying the Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-mediated system can cause the fluorescence quenching of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence detection of OTA
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions (Supporting information, Fig. S10), the dual-modal signals were proposed for OTA basing on multi-colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence strategies. Firstly, the colorimetric detection was realized according to the etching level of Au NRs by observing the color change and LSPR band shifting. As in Fig. 7A and C, by increasing OTA concentrations, the LSPR of Au NRs progressively blue shifted accompanied with vivid color changes (claret → green → blue → grey → thistle). Nevertheless, in Fig. 7E, a linear regression equation between the LSPR peak shift with the logarithm of OTA concentrations can be expressed as Δλ = 84.06 + 23.77[lgC OTA (μg/L)] (R2 = 0.993), while the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.83 ng/L (S/N = 3). Meanwhile, in order to further improve the possibility of practical application of the multi-colorimetric detection of OTA, we fabricated a CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent immunosensor for quantitative measurement of OTA. In Fig. 7B, when the OTA concentration increased, the CDs fluorescence intensity at 424 nm decreased while the fluorescent peak of DAP at 563 nm increased gradually. Simultaneously, the fluorescent color of the solution changed from blue to yellow (Fig. 7D), which was easily recognized by the naked eyes. Concurrently, in Fig. 7F, a linear relationship between I563/I424 and the logarithm of OTA concentrations can be found as I563/I424 = 1.78 + 0.52[lgC OTA (μg/L)] (R2 = 0.992) in the range of 1 ng/L and 10 μg/L, while the LOD was 0.56 ng/L (S/N = 3). These results demonstrated that the dual-modal immunoassay strategy can shoulder the assignment for sensitive and reliable detection of OTA, where the linear range and LOD of this immunosensor were comparable or better than that of other representative methods (Table S1). Additionally, thanks to different signal response pathways, the two signal routes are independent in the detection of OTA. Thus, this dual-mode detection platform can exhibit more intuitive, accurate, and sensitive performance, particularly the wealth of color variations for the multi-colorimetric provides great potential for field detection.

The stability and reproducibility are significant to evaluate the performance of the dual-modal immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 8A-B, the stability was demonstrated by analyzing five concentrations levels of OTA by the same immunoassay. After one week of storage, the developed immunosensor can exhibit satisfied performance, indicating that the proposed immunosensor maintained adequate stability for OTA detection. Then, the reproducibility of the immunosensor was evaluated by analyzing one concentration using three immunosensors, suggesting that there was no significant difference between the three sensors for either multi-colorimetric or ratiometric fluorescent detection.

The complexity of real samples presents a challenge for OTA detection not only in sensitivity but also in specificity. Hence, the interference study was performed using aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), trichothecenes 2 (T-2), deoxynivalenol (DON), and zearalenone (ZEN). As shown in Fig. 8C, the potential interferents exhibited negligible effects on the dual-modality immunoassay platform (both the LSPR peak shifts and the fluorescence intensity ratio of I563/I424), certificating the constructed dual-modal multi-colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent strategy possessed excellent selectivity. Accordingly, to further explore the practicability and reliability of the established dual-modality methods in the actual sample detection, the real samples (the lake water was obtained from the South China Agricultural University, and the millet was purchased from the local supermarket) were spiked with OTA at different concentration including 0.1, 0.01, and 0.001 μg/L via the standard addition method. In Fig. 8D, the recoveries of OTA were in the range of 94.7%–107.8% (multi-colorimetric method) and 94.5%–112.3% (ratiometric fluorescent method), respectively. These results indicated that the dual-modal strategy hold a promising potential to detect OTA in real samples.

In this work, based on the Pearson’s hard and soft acid-base principle, we have successfully developed a facile template-assisted strategy to fabricate Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages. A new dual-modal (multicolorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence) immunoassay for visual and sensitive detection of OTA via different conversion mechanisms and signal transmissions by taking advantage of biomimetic peroxidase of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 yolk-shell nanocages. The properties of the proposed immunosensor can be summarized as following aspects: (1) The excellent peroxidase-like properties of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3 nanocages may be due to the synergistic effect of extraordinary yolk-shell structure, which enormously increased the specific surface area and exposed more active binding sites for improving the sensitivity and the detection range; (2) The multicolor colorimetric strategy was based on Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-catalyzed reaction-mediated etching of Au NRs, and the OTA concentration can be conveniently sensed by naked eyes on the basis of vivid color changes during the etching process. (3) DAP was the oxidation product of Cu2O@Fe(OH)3-catalyzed reaction of OPD with H2O2. Due to the inner filter effect between DAP and CDs, the ratiometric fluorescence platform for quantitative analysis of OTA was also developed, thus making the fluorescent detection with outstanding self-calibration ability and excellent accuracy. In view of these advantages, the construction of dual-modal immunoassay not only has promising potential for the OTA detection in real samples but also can be extended to detect other food contaminants by changing the corresponding biomolecules.

Hongshuai Zhu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Yue Cai: Investigation, Methodology. Aori Qileng: Investigation, Methodology. Zhu Quan: Investigation, Methodology. Wei Zeng: Investigation, Methodology. Kaiyu He: Visualization, Methodology. Yingju Liu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
